coredata.dc:description: Small-sized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are gaining increasing attention in the field of biomedical applications. The environmental and biological compatibility of positively charged CDs has been extensively investigated; however, the potential cytotoxicity caused by negatively and particularly neutrally charged small CDs has been significantly overlooked. In this study, we conducted a comprehensive investigation into the cellular membrane disruption effect of weakly negatively charged 3-nm CDs using a combination of various biophysical techniques. Our findings demonstrate that even at a low concentration of 0.5 μg mL−1, these CDs induce significant perturbations on the cellular membrane, resulting in increased membrane permeability due to asymmetric disruption of the bilayer structure. Furthermore, CDs exhibit distinct mechanisms at different concentrations, including prompt insertion into the bilayer at low concentrations (<20 μg mL−1) and a synergistic effect after a threshold time at high concentrations (e.g., 25–200 μg mL−1). Moreover, these CDs possess specific antibacterial properties against Acinetobacter baumannii (with a minimum inhibitory concentration of 50 μg mL−1) while showing minimal hemolytic or cytotoxic effects on mammalian cells. This study provides comprehensive insights into the biophysical aspects of cellular membrane toxicity caused by small weakly negatively charged CDs and contributes to assessing their potential biomedical applications.

(e-h) Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, e), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, f), UV–visible absorption spectroscopy (g), as well as fluorescent excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectra analysis (h).

(b) Membrane disruption by CDs (Top: top-view snapshot of the membrane lipid tails and the hole showing the penetration of CDs; Bottom: Voronoi tessellation of the outer leaflet of the bilayer). Arrows indicate the addition time of different solution (small unilamellar vesicles, SUVs, at 0.3 mg mL−1, ultrapure water, or CDs at 50 μg mL−1) using a peristaltic pump at a flow rate of 10 µL min−1 and 25 ℃.

Synchrotron radiation X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the CD-lipid composite structure. (a) The z-direction period evolution of a dry DOPC multilayered film after applying 0.02 mL of CD dispersion (at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1) onto the film surface. Pure water was used as a control to assess membrane swelling, while melittin (20 μg mL−1) served as a reference system. Inset shows the definition of z-spacing, d, and in-plane spacing, a. (b-c) XRD profiles for films composed of mixtures of DOPC lipids and varying concentrations of CD or melittin in the z-direction or x-y plane. The pristine solution contained lipids (at a concentration of 200 μg mL−1), CDs ranging from 1 to 50 μg mL−1, or melittin at concentrations of either 0.3 or 5 μg mL−1. A pure DOPC film was used as a control. (d, e) Corresponding values of d and a determined from the XRD data analysis. Three independent experiments were conducted, and the mean ± SD is presented here. P-values were calculated by t-test (*p < 0.05; **p < 0.01; *** p < 0.001). (f) A schematic representation illustrating the CD-lipid composite film.

The impact of CDs on the electrical properties of SLBs. (a) Schematic diagram of the photo-voltage test system, illustrating a phospholipid bilayer membrane supported on a silicon wafer surface. The hydrophilic headgroups and hydrophobic alkyl tails of phospholipids are shown as purple beads and gray lines, respectively. The photoelectrode is illuminated by a square-wave modulated light, which stimulates photoinduced charges as a square-wave voltage input of the system (as shown in b). (a) Histograms depicting the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC50) of CDs against bacterial strains, including Acinetobacter baumannii, Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Polymyxin B-resistant A. baumannii (PRAB), Polymyxin B-resistant E. coli (PRE. coli), Methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) (highlighted in red), as well as the half cytotoxic concentration (HC50) and half inhibitory concentration (IC50) of CDs against red blood cells (RBCs), SV40 transformed kidney cells from African green monkey (COS-7) and human gastric carcinoma cells (MGC803) (highlighted in blue). (b) Agar hole diffusion-inhibition zone method evaluating the antibacterial effect of CDs against E. coli. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and graphene oxide nanosheets (GO) were used as controls. (c) LIVE/DEAD staining images demonstrating the effects of CDs or GO treatment at their 2 × MIC for 12 h on AB and E. coli. All bacteria are shown in green while dead bacteria are depicted in red. Scale bar represents 10 µm. (d) Representative SEM images exhibiting the morphological changes induced by incubation with CDs on E. coli and S. aureus. The areas displaying significant damage are indicated by white arrows. The data in (a) was based on 3 independent experiments, and the mean ± SD is shown.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Small-sized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are gaining increasing attention in the field of biomedical applications. The environmental and biological compatibility of positively charged CDs has been extensively investigated; however, the potential cytotoxicity caused by negatively and particularly neutrally charged small CDs has been significantly overlooked. In this study, we conducted a comprehensive investigation into the cellular membrane disruption effect of weakly negatively charged 3-nm CDs using a combination of various biophysical techniques. Our findings demonstrate that even at a low concentration of 0.5 μg mL−1, these CDs induce significant perturbations on the cellular membrane, resulting in increased membrane permeability due to asymmetric disruption of the bilayer structure. Furthermore, CDs exhibit distinct mechanisms at different concentrations, including prompt insertion into the bilayer at low concentrations (<20 μg mL−1) and a synergistic effect after a threshold time at high concentrations (e.g., 25–200 μg mL−1). Moreover, these CDs possess specific antibacterial properties against Acinetobacter baumannii (with a minimum inhibitory concentration of 50 μg mL−1) while showing minimal hemolytic or cytotoxic effects on mammalian cells. This study provides comprehensive insights into the biophysical aspects of cellular membrane toxicity caused by small weakly negatively charged CDs and contributes to assessing their potential biomedical applications.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Dynamic giant unilamellar vesicle leakage assay

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of nanomaterials, particularly engineered nanoparticles (NPs), in the biomedical field for applications such as diagnostic imaging, drug delivery, and gene therapy has experienced significant expansion in recent years [1–4]. Considerable attention is dedicated to the development of novel NPs with enhanced performances. Among these, fluorescent semiconductor core/shell quantum dots possess several advantages including their small size (1–5 nm in diameter), high brightness, and excellent photostability [5–7]. However, their potential toxicity in living organisms remains a major limitation to the use of quantum dots, thereby impeding their clinical research and application [8]. Recently, surface-passivated carbon dots (CDs) have been developed, exhibiting robust photoluminescence and finding applications in the field of biomedicine [9–12]. In contrast to semiconductor materials, carbon-based materials derived from biodegradable polymers or lipids demonstrate superior environmental and biological compatibility. Moreover, compared to other carbon-based nanoparticles such as graphite and carbon nanotubes, CDs possess enhanced hydrophilicity and renal clearance size, which may enhance their bioavailability and adaptability in vivo [13,14].

Despite this, there is a substantial body of research investigating the potential cytotoxicity of carbon nanomaterials before their clinical application becomes possible [9, 15–19]. Factors such as size [17, 20–26], charge [16, 27–29], and dosage [15, 17, 30] play a crucial role in determining the cytotoxic effects of nanomaterials. When compared to other forms like carbon fibers or single/multi-walled carbon nanotubes [26], or graphite-based materials such as graphene or graphene oxide (GO) [25], it has been observed that CDs smaller than 6 nm exhibit lower toxicity levels due to efficient elimination through renal clearance pathways [17]. It has been noted that these small-sized CDs possess the ability to transmigrate across endothelial cell monolayers, enabling them to bypass the circulatory system [17]. Additionally, surface properties including charged states also play a crucial role in particle-cell interactions. Positively charged CDs consistently demonstrate stronger perturbation effects on cells due to the negatively charged nature of cell membranes and certain biological entities (e.g., nucleic acids, proteins). In contrast, negatively charged CDs show lower toxicity compared to their positively charged counterparts, while neutrally charged CDs exhibit reduced toxicity relative to negatively charged ones [31]. This point was confirmed by the experiment conducted by Havrdova et al., in which they compared the effects of positively charged polyethylenimine CDs, negatively charged candle soot-derived CDs, and neutrally charged polyethyleneglycol (PEG) CDs on mouse fibroblast morphology, intracellular transport, and cell cycle function [16]. The study revealed that the positively charged CDs displayed high toxicity due to their significant migration into the nucleus. At a concentration of 50 μg mL−1, these CDs induced notable alterations in cell morphology and significantly disrupted the cell cycle. On the other hand, for negatively charged CDs, morphological changes in cells occurred at higher doses (200 μg mL−1), while significant abnormalities in the cell cycle required an even higher dose of 350 μg mL−1. In comparison, neutrally charged CDs exhibited minimal toxicity. The findings of Wei et al. also demonstrated that increasing the concentration and incubation time led to enhanced cytotoxicity and induction of apoptosis by positively charged spermine CDs. In contrast, negatively charged candle-soot CDs and uncharged glucose CDs exhibited lower or no toxicity [27]. Briefly, the significance of cytotoxicity in negatively charged, particularly electrically neutral, small-sized CDs has been significantly overlooked.

However, caution should still be exercised when using small-sized negatively or neutrally charged CDs, as cells are a complex integrated system that includes crucial components such as cell membranes. It is worth noting that experimental evidence has demonstrated the interaction between negatively charged NPs and neuronal membranes while positively and neutrally charged NPs do not localize on neurons [32]. In this study, using a combination of multiple biophysical techniques, the cell membrane perturbation effect of weakly negatively charged 3-nm CDs with varying concentrations was meticulously assessed. Unlike previous studies, we found that these CDs still exerted significant perturbations on cell membranes, including asymmetric disruption of the bilayer structure and increased membrane permeability. Notably, even at very low concentrations (e.g., 0.5 μg mL−1), these CDs were able to promptly insert into the membrane and induce permeation. Moreover, live-cell assays demonstrated selective cytotoxicity of these CDs against Acinetobacter baumannii (AB), with minimal hemolytic or cytotoxic effects on mammalian cells such as SV40 transformed kidney cells from African green monkey (COS-7) and human gastric carcinoma cells (MGC803). The present study elucidates the cellular membrane toxicity of small negatively charged CDs from a biophysical standpoint, thereby emphasizing the imperative for cautious utilization of such CDs in clinical applications.

1,2-Dioleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DOPC), 1,2-dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-Phosphoethanolamine-N-(lissamine rhodamine B sulfonyl) (RhB-PE) were purchased from Avanti Polar Lipids. Citric acid, penicillamine, and cellulose dialysis bags (500 −1000 Da) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co. Ltd. GO nanosheets were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. Calcein and melittin were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All experiments were performed at the room temperature of 25 °C unless otherwise stated.

Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs were prepared by thermal polymerization using citric acid and penicillamine as reaction precursors. The procedure was conducted as follows: 2 g of citric acid was subjected to carbonization at a temperature of 180 °C for a duration of 50 min. Subsequently, 1.2 g of penicillamine was added to sustain the reaction under the same temperature conditions for an additional period of 30 min. Following completion of the reaction, 20 mL of ultrapure water was introduced for dissolution and allowed to cool down to room temperature. The resulting solution underwent filtration using a pinhead filter with a pore size of 0.22 µm to eliminate any insoluble precipitates present in the water phase. Ultimately, the crude product underwent further purification through filtration and dialysis (500 −1000 MW), leading to the successful synthesis of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs.

The synthesized CDs were structurally characterized using a FEI/Philips Tecnai G2 F20 TWIN transmission electron microscope (TEM) to obtain TEM and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained via a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. A Bruker Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR; Hyperion) for FTIR spectrum analysis. Perkin Elmer UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750) was utilized for UV-Vis absorption measurements. The Horiba Jobin Yvon (Fluoro Max-4) photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer was adopted to acquire PL spectra. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement was conducted using a ZEN3690 zetasizer from Malvern, U.K.

The GUVs were prepared utilizing the conventional electroformation technique. In brief, a mixture comprising DOPC (2 mg mL−1) and RhB-PE dissolved in chloroform at a molar ratio of 200:1 was spin-coated onto an ITO glass substrate and subsequently subjected to vacuum drying for no less than two hours. Following this step, an energizing process involving alternate voltage (0.5 V × 20 min, 1.0 V × 20 min, and 1.5 V × 3 h) along with introduction of 100 mM sucrose solution took place over three consecutive hours duration period. The resulting vesicles were harvested by centrifugation (8000 rpm × 20 min) within the size range of 8–30 µm for subsequent experiments. The obtained GUVs were dispersed in a self-made chamber containing 100 mM glucose, followed by the addition of calcein solution as a marker for membrane leakage. Once the system reached stability, varying concentrations of CDs ranging from 0.5 to 200 μg mL−1 were carefully injected into the vesicle solution. In situ optical observations were conducted using an inverted confocal laser scanning microscope (LSM710, Zeiss) equipped with a 63 × oil objective. Fluorescence signals in the green channel (EX 488 nm, EM BP 530/50 nm) and red (EX 561 nm, EM575–640 nm) channels was collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation (QCM-D) monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: The supported phospholipid bilayer (SLB) was fabricated on a QCM-D crystal coated with silica using the conventional method [33,34]. Briefly, DOPC lipids were dissolved in a chloroform solution (3.0 mg mL−1), and 0.1 mL of the solution was completely evaporated in a clean glass bottle under a slow stream of nitrogen gas, followed by overnight placement in a vacuum drying oven. Subsequently, 1 mL of ultrapure water was added and sonicated for approximately 45 min. The resulting mixture was then extruded 21 times through a polycarbonate membrane with pores measuring 100 nm in size (Avanti Polar Lipids). The prepared small unilamellar vesicles (SUVs, 0.3 mg mL−1) were used immediately.

The QCM-D measurements were conducted using a Q-sense E4 instrument. A naked SiO2 sensor was utilized as the substrate, and different solutions were sequentially injected at a flow rate of 10 µL min−1 and a temperature of 25 ℃ using a peristaltic pump. Real-time changes in resonance frequency (f) and energy dissipation (D) at various harmonics (from 5th to 11th in our experiments) were recorded. Prior to each test, the crystal was immersed in a 2 wt% SDS solution for 30 min, followed by rinsing with ample ultrapure water. Subsequently, it was dried using a slow stream of nitrogen and treated with ozone for 30 min before settling down. To ensure system stability, at least 30 min of ultrapure water circulation was performed prior to the experiment to establish a stable baseline. The ultrapure water was then replaced with the sample solution, and real-time recordings of f and D were obtained.

Using the conventional drop-coating method, a volume of 50 µL of DOPC phospholipids was precisely deposited onto a bare silicon substrate to form a dry multilayer membrane with an area measuring 1 cm2 [35]. To prepare the composite multilayer membrane consisting of DOPC lipids and CDs, 0.1 mL of chloroform-dissolved DOPC (2 mg mL−1) lipid was placed in a clean glass bottle and allowed to completely evaporate. Subsequently, 1 mL of ultrapure water containing CDs was added to the glass bottle and subjected to ultrasonication for approximately 45 min. The resulting vesicle aqueous solution was then drop-coated onto substrates for the fabrication of lipid-CD composite multilayer film.

Synchrotron XRD measurements were conducted at Synchrotron Radiation Source BL02U2 in Shanghai, China. The incident X-ray wavelength was 1.25 Å, the distance between the sample and the CCD (pixels, 172 ×172) was 230.2774 mm, and the incident angle was set to 0.4°. Following the acquisition of the initial structure of the multilayer phospholipid membrane, CD dispersion, melittin solution, or ultrapure water were added at varying concentrations to monitor dynamic changes in membrane ordering. The radial intensity of obtained 2D powder patterns was integrated using FIT2D software (integration range, Z direction: 75–105; X-direction: 1–20), generating an average profile of one-dimensional intensity (I) versus 2θ angle. The period d can be determined using a specific equation
2 d sin
θ
= n λ
where
n = 1 , 2 , 3 …
represents the first, second, and third peak, respectively.

Escherichia coli CGMCC 1.12883 and Acinetobacter baumannii ATCC 19606 were employed as model microorganisms to investigate drug resistance. Sublethal doses of the antimicrobial agent Polymyxin B were repeatedly administered to induce drug resistance in both E. coli and A. baumannii (or AB). The sub-Minimal Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) concentrations (1/8 × MIC) exposed E. coli and AB were regrown to logarithmic growth phase, with MIC measured and used for subsequent passages. The MIC of Polymyxin B against E. coli and AB was tested over 14 passages, ultimately resulting in the emergence of Polymyxin B-resistant E. coli (PRE. coli) and Polymyxin B-resistant AB (PRAB).

The MIC of CDs was determined by the standard broth microdilution method. E. coli CGMCC 1.12883, A. baumannii ATCC 19606, Staphylococcus aureus CGMCC 1.10755, Methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA), PRE. coli and PRAB were grown to midlogarithmic phase and diluted to an optical density of approximately 0.1 at a 600 nm wavelength. A volume of 100 µL broth, containing 106 CFU mL−1 of bacteria, was added to each well of the 96-well plates. CDs was added to a final concentration of 0–1280 µg mL−1 by gradient. The optical density readings of microbial solutions were monitored by measuring OD 600 nm at 12 h. The lowest concentration that kills 50% of the bacteria, as observed with the microplate reader (Infinite F50, TECAN), was recorded as the MIC50 value. All MIC measurements were the average of more than three independent experiments.

The antimicrobial activity of CDs and graphene oxide (GO), a carbon nanomaterial known for its potential antibacterial properties with a size of 100 nm (Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd), was evaluated using the agar well diffusion method. Approximately 1 mL of bacterial dispersion (106 CFU mL−1) and 20 mL of sterilized agar medium were dispensed into petri dishes using a pipette gun. After solidification, three separate wells with an approximate diameter of 6 mm were created in the agar medium. Subsequently, 50 µL PBS, GO solution (10.24 mg mL−1), and CD solution (10.24 mg mL−1) were added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Following incubation, the diameters (mm) of the inhibition zones were measured.

The live and dead cell staining test was conducted according to the conventional method. E. coli and AB (106 CFU mL−1) were incubated at 37 °C in the presence of CDs or GO. Untreated E. coli and AB were used as control groups. After 12 h of incubation, the bacteria were harvested through centrifugation, followed by resuspension in PBS and staining with propidium iodide (PI) or DAPI at room temperature for 30 min. The stained bacteria were carefully washed and suspended twice in 1 mL of PBS solution. Subsequently, a uniform distribution of 50 µL bacterial suspension was achieved in each sample placed on clean glass slides for in situ observation using an inverted confocal microscope system (LSM 710, Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) equipped with a 63 × oil objective. All images were captured under consistent instrumental settings.

E. coli and S. aureus were treated with CDs (at 5 × MIC concentrations) and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 2 h at room temperature. Then dehydrated with a series of ethanol solutions with different volume contents (35%, 50%, 70%, 90%, 95% and 100%). Finally, coated with gold and imaged with a scanning electron microscope (SEM; SU8010, HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan).

The hemolysis assay was conducted according to a previously reported method [36]. The red blood cell suspension was treated with CDs (up to 640 μg mL−1) for 2 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged at 1800 g for 5 min, and the optical density (OD) value of the supernatant at 550 nm was measured. Ultrapure water was used as a positive control, while PBS served as the negative control.

The traditional MTT assay was performed to assess the cytotoxicity of CDs [36,37]. Briefly, SV40 transformed kidney cells from African green monkey (COS-7) and human gastric cancer (MGC-803) cells were cultured until they reached the mid-logarithmic growth phase. The cells were then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min and seeded into a 96-well plate (∼104 per mL, with each well containing 100 µL). After overnight incubation to allow cell adhesion, CDs were added at concentrations up to 1200 µg mL−1, while DMEM served as the control. The cells were co-incubated with the test agents for either 24 or 48 h. Subsequently, they were treated with MTT solution (5 mg mL−1 × 20 µL) for an additional 4 h. After centrifugation at 3000 rpm for another 10 min, the precipitates were resuspended in DMSO (100 µL), and their absorbance was measured at a wavelength of 490 nm using a microplate reader.

All experimental and simulation measurements were performed with at least three to five replicates for each condition/data point. Analysis of variance was performed using OriginPro 9.0 (OriginLab software, Northampton, MA) and values are displayed as mean ± standard deviation. Data set of experiment was performed with samples compared to controls unless otherwise stated. P-values were calculated by t-test (*p < 0.05; **p < 0.01; *** p < 0.001).

The sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs were synthesized via thermal polymerization using citric acid and penicillamine as precursors ( Fig. 1a) [38]. TEM and DLS techniques were employed to characterize the morphology and size distribution of the CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1b, the CDs exhibited uniform spheroidal morphology with particle sizes ranging from 1–5 nm (with a mean size of 2.7 nm) according to DLS particle size analysis (Fig. 1c). High-resolution TEM images shown in the inset revealed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm for the CDs, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, indicating their graphitic structure and excellent crystallinity. The zeta potential of the CDs was approximately − 0.5 mV, as depicted in Fig. 1d. The CDs maintained weakly negative electrical properties even after continuous exposure to light (e.g., exposure to a 300 W xenon lamp for a duration of 30 min).

Furthermore, the structural and compositional analysis of CDs was conducted using FTIR and XPS, as depicted in Figs. 1e, f and S1, along with the Corresponding Note in the Supporting Information. The FTIR and XPS spectra suggest that the CDs are co-doped with sulfur and nitrogen, exhibiting various functional groups on their surface including carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl, sulfhydryl, and amino. The UV–vis absorption spectra revealed that the CDs exhibited absorption peaks at 212 nm, 240 nm, and 352 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 1g. Among these, the absorption peak 210 nm can be attributed to the retention of the characteristic absorption peak of penicillamine, which is a constituent of the raw material. The other two peaks correspond to π-π * and n-π * transitions within CDs. Additionally, we investigated the fluorescence characteristics of CDs (Fig. 1h). The fluorescence spectra demonstrated an optimal emission peak at a wavelength of 470 nm when excited at 410 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration- and time-dependent membrane permeabilization by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The potential disruptions of CDs in the structure of lipid bilayer membranes can be investigated using dynamic GUV leakage assays, which involve real-time monitoring of the transmembrane leakage of fluorescent small molecule dyes caused by agent actions [39,40]. Accordingly, we prepared GUVs using DOPC labeled with 0.5 mol% RhB-PE and dispersed them in a calcein solution. The CD dispersion, at various concentrations, was added at the initial time point (∼time point zero), and the process of calcein transmembrane transport into individual GUVs was recorded in real-time to reflect the disturbance caused by CDs to the membrane and resulting membrane permeabilization.

The GUVs remained stable for over four hours during confocal observation in the absence of CDs, without any entry of calcein. However, the addition of CDs at a low concentration of 0.5 μg mL−1 resulted in transmembrane entry of calcein, indicating membrane permeabilization even at such a low concentration (Figs. 2a and S2). Calcein entry reached a plateau after approximately one hour. Increasing CD concentration to 1 or 10 μg mL−1 significantly accelerated this process, fluorescent intensity within GUVs reaching saturation after only 8–15 min. Moreover, CDs at a concentration range of 5–15 μg mL−1 resulted in the collapse of approximately 10% of observed GUVs within a five-minute timeframe (indicated by the red dashed rectangle in Figs. 2a and S2a-c).

However, with further enhancement of the CD concentration, the entry of calcein slowed down. For instance, at a CD concentration of 25 μg mL−1, the entry of calcein reaches saturation after approximately 40 min. Higher concentrations such as 50, 100, or 200 μg mL−1 did not accelerate the entry of calcein. To quantitatively evaluate the membrane permeabilization efficiency of CDs at different concentrations, time-dependent fluorescence intensity profiles (indicating calcein content) within individual vesicles were acquired, which are representatively shown in Fig. 2b. Additionally, the mean rate of calcein entry was calculated as 80% divided by the time required for achieving an 80% increase in fluorescence intensity within individual vesicles (Fig. 2c). It is intriguing to observe that, within the range of 0.5 to 10 μg mL−1, there is a positive correlation between CD concentration and the mean entry rate of calcein, suggesting an enhanced efficiency in membrane permeabilization. However, in the concentration range of 25–200 μg mL−1, the mean entry rate remains relatively constant, although with strong deviations among individual GUVs, indicating a low and unvarying membrane penetration efficiency for high concentrations of CDs. It is intriguing to observe that, at a concentration of 20 μg mL−1, the GUVs exhibited two distinct modes (Fig. S2d, e, g and Corresponding Note in the Supporting Information). Some of the GUVs demonstrated immediate permeation, while others remained stable until reaching a threshold time point. This indicates that the concentration serves as a critical transition point for both mechanisms.

In general, two noteworthy phenomena can be observed. Firstly, within the concentration range of 0.5–5 μg mL−1, there is a linear increase in fluorescence intensity of calcein over time (black, red, and blue profiles in Fig. 2b). This indicates an immediate permeabilization of the membrane upon addition of CDs, with no change in the extent of permeability over time. On the other hand, within 25 to 200 μg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity exhibits an S-pattern characterized by a relatively slow increase followed by rapid escalation until reaching saturation, which can be fitted with a sigmoidal equation of
y =
a
1 + exp
− k ×
x −
x
c
. This suggests that CD's effect on membrane permeability undergoes an accumulation process until surpassing a certain critical threshold [40]. The calculation of the Stable Entry Rate, k, of calcein during the linear growth stage is illustrated in Fig. 2d. At concentrations ranging from 25–200 μg mL−1, this value remains comparable to that at 5 μg mL−1, which indicates that the high concentration CDs can rapidly induce significant membrane penetration once the critical moment is surpassed. Secondly, confocal images of GUVs revealed that the addition of low concentration CDs promptly resulted in the appearance of calcein (i.e., green fluorescence circle) co-localized with the membrane (marked with red arrows in Fig. 2a and S3), accompanied by transmembrane entry of calcein. In contrast, only after a critical time does the addition of high concentration CD lead to the emergence of the green circle, followed by a sudden influx and saturation within minutes with a large amount of calcein (corresponding to the aforementioned linear growth stage). In summary, our results suggest that the addition of a low concentration CDs immediately induces lipid packing defects and permeability changes that remain constant over time; conversely, the introduction of a high concentration of CDs initially does not affect membrane integrity obviously until reaching a critical moment when numerous defects suddenly emerge, leading to significant membrane permeation. The transition point for both mechanisms is observed at approximately 20 μg mL−1. These observations collectively indicate the complicated membrane disrupting mechanism of CDs, which varies at low and high concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations of the CD-membrane interactions
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the molecular mechanism underlying distinct interaction processes between CDs and cell membranes at low and high CD concentrations, MD simulations were performed. MD has been widely recognized as a powerful tool for investigating the intricate molecular details of membrane-related biological activities [4,41]. In this study, a model of a cell membrane was constructed using a DOPC lipid bilayer in the MD simulations, while CDs, fabricated in accordance to the experimental measurements, with varying numbers were positioned above the membrane. The weakly negatively charged CD particle was designed by arranging C2-type beads based on a face-centered cubic (FCC) lattice with a nearest distance of 0.37 nm to form a spherical shape as previously reported [42]; Additionally, hydrophobic C2-type beads and hydrophilic P1-type beads covered the surface of the particle core randomly in equal proportions (50% each), with 10% of these P1-type beads being randomly replaced by Qa-type beads (negatively charged). The diameter of the CD particle is approximately 2.7 nm.

As depicted in
Fig. 3a, in the case of single CD, the particle swiftly translocated into the membrane and predominantly embedded itself within the upper monolayer; maintaining this interaction configuration consistently throughout the entire simulation process (of 5 μs). Moreover, there was a slight disruption observed in the local membrane lipids around the embedded particle (Fig. 3b). However, when multiple CDs were present (e.g., 5 or 8 NPs), there were changes in the membrane disruption scenarios. As depicted in Fig. 3a, CDs aggregated into clusters and hindered their insertion into the membrane. The CD aggregate adsorbed onto the surface for a certain period of time (e.g., for 5 NPs, this period is approximately 3000 ns as shown in Fig. 3a) before suddenly penetrating into it. Moreover, compared to a single CD, these NPs significantly intensified the degree of membrane disruption (Fig. 3b). Such a “step-like” manner can be clearly demonstrated by observing the evolution of insertion depth of CDs into membrane as well as interaction energy between CDs and lipids (Fig. 3c, d). For a single CD, easy membrane insertion is observed; however, when multiple CDs are present, initial surface binding occurs without penetration. Nevertheless, during this period, strong interactions between CDs and the membrane are observed (blue and green arrows in Fig. 3d). Consequently, sudden membrane penetration by CDs takes place after an incubation time. These simulation results contribute to our understanding of experimental observations and provide insights into the varying mechanisms underlying NP-membrane interactions at different concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and property perturbation of membrane by CDs investigated by QCM-D, Synchrotron XRD and photo-voltage monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further elucidate the structural perturbation of CDs on lipid bilayer membranes, a QCM-D experiment was conducted. Employing a naked SiO2 sensor as the substrate, different solutions were injected in sequence using a peristaltic pump (at a flow rate of 10 µL min−1 and temperature of 25 ℃) and real-time changes in resonance frequency, f, and energy dissipation, D, at various harmonics (from 5th to 11th), were recorded. These measurements can provide insights into the dynamic alterations in mass and viscoelasticity at the sensor surface. Generally, f decreases with the added mass and vice versa. A higher D indicates the layer on the surface becomes thicker and/or softer; conversely, it becomes more rigid and compact [33,34].

A conventional approach for constructing SLBs on silica chip surfaces was applied by using 100 nm DOPC vesicles [33]. We observed that the addition of SUV solution (at 0.3 mg mL−1) led to a continuous decrease in f, eventually reaching an equilibrium at − 22 Hz; simultaneously, the value of D exhibited a progressive increase followed by a decline, ultimately reaching a small value and achieving equilibrium after 150 min; moreover, there was minimal disparity observed in f and D across different overtones. The characteristic signatures of f and D signify the formation of a high-quality SLB film characterized by structural symmetry between its upper and lower leaflets [43].

After the injection of distilled water for a thorough cleaning, we introduced CD dispersion (at 50 μg mL−1) into the system. The evolution of f and D can be categorized into three stages: an initial significant decrease in f (Stage I), followed by a slight increase leading to stability (Stage II), and ultimately reaching a stable value of approximately − 24 Hz (Stage III). Simultaneously, there is a notable increase in the D value, which subsequently undergoes rapid decline before attaining stability. Additionally, f values at different overtones overlap well, whereas those of D display considerable disparity; specifically, D at lower overtones (e.g., D5) are considerably larger than higher overtones (e.g., D11). In Stage I, the decrease in f indicates an increase in SLB mass, suggesting the adsorption of CD on the membrane surface. This is further supported by the significant increase in D during this process. At Stage II, a slight increase in f and a significant decrease in D suggest a decrease in the mass of lipid-CD composite, possibly due to the removal of CDs or CD-lipid complex from the sensor surface. Note that mass removal may have already occurred during Stage I, although this stage seems to be primarily characterized by mass increase.

At Stage III, the final f-value remains smaller than that of the original pure SLB, indicating that the total mass of the remaining lipid-CD structure still exceeds that of the pure membrane. Moreover, the final D value significantly surpasses that of the pure SLB state, suggesting a severe disruption in membrane structure and substantial damage to the lipid packing state. Note that the f and D values at different harmonics can provide three-dimensional information of the surface layer, as the penetration depth of the harmonic wave is inversely proportional to its frequency. That is, a higher harmonic probe closer to the sensor surface than a lower harmonic [34]. In our experiment, we observed a significantly higher value of D5 compared to D11, suggesting a lower order in the upper leaflet relative to the lower leaflet. This implies that the lipid packing state in the upper leaflet is less compact. These findings indicate an asymmetric structural damage to the bilayer membrane. In conclusion, QCM-D experiments demonstrated substantial asymmetrical structural disruption in both leaflets of the lipid membrane caused by CD adsorption.

Synchrotron XRD is a powerful technique for real-time characterization of the lipid assembly, enabling the investigation of both the lamellar in the z-direction and the arrangement of lipid tail chains in the x-y plane [35]. In this study, we prepared a dry multilayered film of 1 cm2 area on a bare silicon substrate using the classical drop-coating method. The film consisted of approximately 31 lipid bilayers composed of DOPC. Subsequently, a volume of 0.02 mL CD dispersion (at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1) was dropped onto the film to monitor dynamic changes in its ordered structure. Pure water was used as a control to observe membrane swelling effects, while melittin peptide at a concentration of 20 μg mL−1, served as a reference system. It is well-known that melittin can adsorb onto the membrane surface leading to thinning and expansion in area, ultimately resulting in toroidal pore formation within the membrane [44–47].

The original dry film, as depicted in Fig. 5a and S4, exhibits a well-ordered structure characterized by a lamellar arrangement along the z-axis with a period of d ≈ 4.6 nm. Additionally, the lipid tails are orderly arranged within the x-y plane with a constant value of a ≈ 4.6 Å. Upon introduction of pure water, CD dispersion or melittin solution, the diffraction peak in the x-y plane rapidly diminishes, indicating membrane swelling and consequent reduction in lipid tail ordering. Regarding the layered structure along the z-axis, distinct variations are observed among these three systems. Specifically, addition of pure water leads to gradual expansion of d from its initial value of 4.6 nm to 5.6 nm within four minutes followed by equilibrium (grey rectangles in Fig. 5a). This corresponds to the swelling process of the multilayer film, and the water layer between the hydrophilic head layer thickens. The addition of melittin solution resulted in a simultaneous increase in d, which decreased to 4.3 nm in 5–11 min and gradually recovered to 5.3 nm in 13–17 min and remained basically stable (blue triangles in Fig. 5a). This trend is consistent with previous expectations: the binding of melittin on the membrane surface will lead to the thinning of the membrane and the extension of the membrane area, and the final penetration of melittin into the membrane can partially release this pressure, resulting in the basic recovery of the membrane thickness.

In contrast, the addition of CD dispersion resulted in distinct changes in d (red circles in Fig. 5a). Following an initial swelling process lasting approximately seven minutes, the d value slightly decreased (from 5.6 nm to 5.3 nm) before quickly returning to its original value. Compared with melittin (at a concentration of 20 μg mL−1; please refer to the Supporting Information for concentration adoption), CDs (at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1) caused significantly less thinning of the lipid film, thereby confirming lower adsorption levels of CDs on the film.

In order to mitigate the impact of swelling on lipid packing, we pre-mix the lipids with a solution of CDs at a lipid concentration of 200 μg mL−1, while varying CDs concentrations from 1.0 to 50.0 μg mL−1. Subsequently, dry composite films are prepared through drop-coating and the influence of CDs content on lipid packing is assessed using Synchrotron XRD analysis. The experimental results demonstrate that these films exhibit excellent ordering in both x-y and z directions across this wide range of CDs concentrations (Fig. 5b, c). Specifically, as the CDs concentration increases from 1.0 μg mL−1 to 50.0 μg mL−1, the distance between lipid tails, a, in the x-y plane expands from 4.6 Å to 5.0 Å, while the period, d, in the z direction extends from 4.6 nm to 4.9 nm (Fig. 5d, e). It should be noted that the perforating peptide melittin caused a completely different trend. These phenomena indicate that, in contrast to melittin molecules which form pores when inserted into the membrane, CD particles can penetrate the lipid layer, resulting in an increased z-direction period in the multilayer film, and a dispersed distribution of lipids in the x-y plane with a greater distance between lipid tails (Fig. 5f).

The disruption of the phospholipid membrane structure by CDs also affects its electrical properties, which are crucial for maintaining various biochemical processes at the interface of the cell membrane. Recently, we have developed a photo-voltage transient technique ( Fig. 6a) that enables real-time tracking of the charging and discharging processes of charges in a phospholipid membrane (i.e., the electric double layer at the interface between the phospholipid membrane and water) [48]. The charges are generated by a semiconductor silicon wafer upon exposure to square-wave modulated light, and the silicon-supported membrane can be considered as a parallel combination of a capacitor and a resistor whose values vary with the electrical properties of the lipid membrane (Fig. 6b). In this work, we prepared a DOPC SLB on a silicon wafer and sealed off the non-film covered area with an insulating adhesive. The SLB was immersed in water and exhibited a characteristic dielectric charge-discharge curve under square-wave modulated light (250 ms ON, 500 ms OFF), and remained stable over time for at least 1 h. Various concentrations of CDs were introduced into the solution, enabling us to record real-time changes in charge and discharge curves. It is evident that low concentration CDs (e.g., ≤5 μg mL−1) have minimal impact (Fig. 6c). However, at a concentration of 15 μg mL−1, the membrane experiences significant overcharging (and over-discharging; Fig. 6d). This phenomenon becomes more pronounced at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1 (Fig. 6e). The absence of SLBs in reference experiments does not yield a similar occurrence, suggesting that the incorporation of CDs into the bilayer membrane is responsible for this overcharging phenomenon. The overcharging (and over-discharging) phenomenon can be attributed to the transfer of charges between the substrate and the conductive CDs embedded in the SLB. Although the underlying mechanism remains unclear, these results confirm the interference effect exerted by CDs to the structure and electrical properties of lipid membranes.

The aforementioned experiments demonstrated that CDs induce both structural and electrical perturbations in lipid membranes. In order to investigate the impact on cellular viability, additional live cell tests were conducted using various strains of bacteria and cell lines. In the experiment, CDs exhibited significant bactericidal activity against various strains of bacteria, including A. baumannii, E. coli, S. aureus, as well as antibiotic-resistant strains such as Methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA), Polymyxin B-resistant A. baumannii (PRAB), and Polymyxin B-resistant E. coli (PRE. coli) ( Fig. 7a). The minimum inhibitory concentration at which 50% growth inhibition occurred (MIC50 values) ranged from 50 to 560 μg mL−1. Notably, CDs demonstrated exceptional efficacy against AB with a remarkably low MIC50 value of 50 μg mL−1, indicating selective antibacterial activity.

The bacteriostatic zone experiment confirms the antibacterial effect of CDs. In this study, we compared the antibacterial efficacy of CDs (at a concentration of 10.24 mg mL−1) with that of Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and GO nanosheets (a carbon nanomaterial known for its potential antibacterial properties; size 100 nm, 3–5 layers, concentration 10.24 mg mL−1). The results clearly demonstrate that CDs exhibit superior bacteria-killing efficacy compared to both PBS and GO, as evidenced by a larger diameter of the bacteriostatic zone measuring 19 mm (Fig. 7b). Fig. 7c presents live and dead cell staining imaging experiments which further highlight the potent bactericidal efficacy of CDs. When compared to samples treated with PBS and GO (concentration 640 μg mL−1 for E. coli and 80 μg mL−1 for AB, i.e., 2 ×MIC, incubation time 12 h), nearly all bacteria exposed to CDs (concentration 640 μg mL−1 for E. coli and 80 μg mL−1 for AB, incubation time 12 h) were eradicated, accompanied by Propidium Iodide (PI) transmembrane entry. The SEM images in Fig. 7d depict the morphological changes and surface holes of E. coli and S. aureus bacteria treated with CDs (at a concentration of 640 μg mL−1 for a treatment time of 12 h), indicating the damaging effect of CDs on bacterial membranes. Interestingly, when visible light irradiation was applied during the CD treatment, the bacteria-killing efficiency of CDs was further enhanced. The concentration-dependent curve in Fig. S5 demonstrates the bactericidal effect of CDs against E. coli, which was achieved by exposing the bacteria to ambient light (or in the dark) at 25 °C for 22 h followed by cultivation at 37 °C for 12 h. The MIC50 value decreased by 77% (from 200 to 45 μg mL−1).

By contrast, CDs exhibit a depressed disrupting effect on red blood cells (RBCs) and mammalian cells such as COS-7 and MGC803, indicating their selective cytotoxicity (Fig. 7a and S6). At concentrations up to 640 μg mL−1, the survival rate of RBCs (incubated for 2 h at 37 °C) and mammalian cells (incubation time: 24 or 48 h) remains above 80%. The toxic thresholds for CDs against these cell types, determined based on a 50% cell mortality threshold, were determined to be above 1200 μg mL−1 (blue histograms in Fig. 7a). These values surpass the MIC50 value for antibacterial activity. Overall, our experiments with live bacteria and cells have yielded intriguing findings: CDs possess antibacterial activity against various bacterial strains, particularly exhibiting potent bactericidal effects against AB pathogens. Furthermore, the bactericidal ability of CDs can be enhanced under light exposure; however, they display lower toxicity towards RBCs and mammalian cells. This phenomenon may be attributed to the selective impact of exposed aromatic amino acids on different types of cell membranes. On a separate note, previous studies have indicated that CDs can undergo photodegradation under specific wavelengths of light, resulting in the formation of toxic molecular structures or the generation of reactive oxygen species [49]. However, photodegradation typically occurs over an extended time period, such as several days. Additionally, the potential impact of light wavelength is also a plausible influencing factor. These aspects have not yet been definitively elucidated.

By employing a diverse range of biophysical techniques, we conducted a comprehensive investigation into the cellular membrane toxicity of weakly negatively charged small CDs across a wide concentration range, which has been significantly overlooked in previous studies. Initially, we synthesized weakly charged CDs with a zeta potential of − 0.5 mV and an approximate size of 3 nm through thermal polymerization of citric acid and Tyr amino acids. The dynamic GUV leakage assay revealed that even at a low concentration of 0.5 μg mL−1, CDs can induce permeation in neutral PC bilayer membranes and transmembrane leakage of calcein. Furthermore, distinct membrane-action processes were observed at low and high CD concentrations. Specifically, at low concentrations (i.e., ≤10 μg mL−1), CDs promptly disrupt the lipid packing state in the membrane, resulting in membrane permeation. This process exhibits a positive concentration dependence. Conversely, the addition of high concentration CDs (i.e., 25–200 μg mL−1) does not significantly affect the membrane structure initially; however, after a critical period of time, synergistic effects among CDs lead to the rapid emergence of membrane defects and substantial penetration. The process exhibits significant deviation among individual GUVs, but does not show obvious concentration dependence. The transition point for both mechanisms occurs at approximately 20 μg mL−1. MD simulation reveals the rapid incorporation of low-concentration CDs into the bilayer membrane, inducing perturbation to the lipid packing state, while high-concentration CDs synergistically disrupt the membrane in a delayed manner. QCM-D and synchrotron XRD analysis demonstrate that CDs can adsorb a small amount onto the surface of the electrically neutral PC bilayer membranes, leading to asymmetric disturbance in lipid bilayers. In the co-assembly of lipids and CDs, a well-organized layered structure can be formed. Within the concentration range of 1–50 μg mL−1 for CDs (equivalent to a lipid-to-CD ratio of 250:1 to 5:1), both the distance between lipid tails in the x-y plane and the period of the lipid layer structure in the z direction increase with higher CD content. The incorporation of CDs into bilayer membranes disturbs the electric properties of the membrane, resulting in overcharging and over-discharging effects, as revealed by photo-voltage tests. In experiments involving live bacteria and cells, CDs exhibit selective membrane activity and demonstrate potent antibacterial effects against a variety of different strains of bacteria, particularly AB bacteria (with an MIC50 concentration of 50 μg mL−1). Furthermore, visible light irradiation for 12 h enhances the resistance of CDs against E. coli by increasing their antibacterial effect by 77%. Conversely, CDs display minimal hemolytic toxicity or cytotoxicity against mammalian cells such as COS-7 and MGC803 (with a HC50 or IC50 value of above 1200 μg mL−1).

Recent research has proposed alternative methods for in vivo administration of CDs, including oral, suppository, intraocular, intranasal, or aerosol routes in addition to injection [17]. Therefore, it is crucial to comprehend the non-specific interaction and uptake of CDs by different cell types in terms of internalization efficiency and cytotoxicity. This study provides comprehensive insights into the biophysical aspects of cellular membrane perturbation caused by 3-nm weakly negatively charged CDs, thereby contributing to a comprehensive assessment of their potential biomedical applications. The perturbation of the cell membrane caused by nanomaterials does not necessarily result in direct cell death; however, it can impact biological processes at the membrane interface, such as the efficacy of membrane-active proteins and peptides [36,40]. Furthermore, the broad-spectrum antibacterial effect against various bacterial strains including drug-resistant ones and particularly the potent bactericidal effects against A. baumannii suggest the potential utility of these CDs for antimicrobial purposes.

ce:section.ce:para: none.
Supplementary images and corresponding notes (PDF). The Supporting Information is available free of charge via the internet at ….

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained significant attention in the field of biomedicine due to their exceptional performance. In contrast to positively charged CDs, negatively or neutrally charged ones are generally considered non-toxic even at high concentrations. This study offers a comprehensive understanding of the membrane toxicity caused by weakly negatively charged 3-nm CDs using a combination of various biophysical techniques. The CDs induce notable disruptions in cell membranes such as asymmetric disturbance of the bilayer structure and increased membrane permeability, resulting in selective antibacterial effects against bacterial strains like baumannii while exhibiting minimal hemolytic or cytotoxic effects on mammalian cells.
